entry.title: Re: How to Transfer Inspection Results from One SAP Syste...
entry.content:
Below approach should be executed

1. Maintain Distribution Model (T-code: BD64)

  • Create a new model view

  • Add message type: QMISUD (Inspection lot results)

  • Add receiver (ECC 6.0 system)

  • Distribute the model

2. Create Partner Profiles (T-code: WE20)

  • In older ECC: Maintain partner profile for ECC 6.0

  • In ECC 6.0: Maintain partner profile for older ECC

  • Configure the following settings:

    • Partner type: LS (Logical system)

    • Message type: QMISUD

    • Inbound options: Process IDoc immediately

3. Define Logical Systems (T-code: SALE)

  • Define logical systems for both systems

  • Assign client to logical system

4. Port Definition (T-code: WE21)

  • Create transactional RFC port

  • Specify RFC destination to target system

5. Maintain Distribution Rules (T-code: BD79)

  • Filter by plant/material if needed

  • Define distribution scope

Key IDoc Type

  • QMISUD01 - Inspection lot results (change pointers enabled)

  • Automatically triggers when results are recorded (transactions QE51N/QE01)

Testing Steps

  1. In older ECC: Create inspection lot (transactions QA11/QA32)

  2. Record results (transaction QE01)

  3. Check IDoc status (transactions WE02/WE05)

  4. Verify in ECC 6.0: Inspection lot is updated

Critical Checks

  • Verify QMISUD is active in change pointers (transaction BD61)

  • Ensure identical master data exists across both systems

  • Test with one batch first 

Critical SAP Notes for Reference

  • SAP Note 157144: Transfer of inspection results via ALE.

  • SAP Note 34484: Distribution of QM master data (if future synchronization is needed)

entry.title: WM Posting Change 309 – Dynamic Bin Instead of TR-ZONE
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

In SAP WM we are using movement type 309 for posting changes.

For movement type 311, we can activate dynamic bin (Scr.) and use requirement type O (Delivery number). The system then determines the outbound delivery number dynamically for the bin.

However, for movement type 309 the system always proposes TR-ZONE for source and destination, and dynamic bin selection is not possible for both at the same time.

Is it possible in standard WM to determine the bin dynamically based on the outbound delivery number for posting change (309)?
Or is an enhancement (user exit/BADI) required?

If anyone has experience with a custom enhancement for this scenario, please share your approach.

entry.title: How ATP & Advanced ATP (aATP) Supercharge the SAP CPQ Sales Quotation Process
entry.content:
When sales teams prepare quotes, one of the biggest challenges is determining accurate product availability and realistic delivery dates. SAP CPQ excels at configuration, pricing, and quote generation, but to promise delivery with confidence, it must rely on real-time inventory and supply chain insights.

That is exactly where SAP ATP (Available‑to‑Promise) and SAP aATP (Advanced Available‑to‑Promise) become powerful extensions during Sales process purely designed and developed custom Iflow (Not available from SAP CPQ Standard Integration Packages).

This blog explores how ATP/aATP works, how it integrates with SAP CPQ and S/4HANA, and the tangible benefits it brings to the sales quotation cycle.

Available Standard APIs for S/4 HANA Public/Private/Onprem

Standard APIsS/4 HANA Public CloudS/4 HANA OnPrem(Private Cloud)
Classical ATPProduct Availability InfoProduct Availability Info
 Basic Product Availability Info
Basic Product Availability Info
 Advanced Backorder Processing Run  
Advanced ATPAdvanced ATP Check Advanced ATP Check
 Supply Availability Protection Plan  
 Product Allocation Object  
 Product Substitution Control
 
 Product Substitution Group
 
 Plant Substitution Control  
 Storage Location Substitution
 
 Storage Location Substitution Exclusion  
 Storage Location Substitution Group  

1. What Is ATP and aATP?

ATP – Classic Availability Check

ATP checks evaluate whether requested materials are available on a given date based on stock, incoming supply, and existing commitments. Classic ATP is embedded in SAP ERP and S/4HANA

aATP – Advanced, Intelligent Availability Checking

Advanced ATP in S/4HANA brings enhanced capabilities, including:

  • Product Availability Check (PAC)
  • Backorder Processing (BOP)
  • Product Allocation (PAL)
  • Alternative-Based Confirmation (ABC)
    These provide intelligent, real-time confirmations considering multiple locations, constraints, and rules

2. Why ATP/aATP Matters for SAP CPQ

SAP CPQ can generate highly accurate quotes, but without visibility into inventory or supply constraints, quotes may overpromise and damage customer trust. ATP/aATP ensures:

  • Real-time material availability during quoting
  • Accurate delivery dates
  • Feasible commitments, even with complex configurations
  • Optimized supply allocation for high-value customers

As SAP CPQ integrates natively with SAP S/4HANA via custom development, sales reps can access availability checks via APIs such as API_AVAIL_TO_PROMISE_CHECK or during quotation-to-sales-order conversion.

3. How ATP/aATP Integrates with SAP CPQ

3.1 Custom Development SAP CPQ–S/4HANA Integration

SAP provides predefined CPQ integration with S/4HANA via BTP Cloud Integration, enabling exchange of configuration, pricing, and master data. A custom Iflow needs to be designed, developed and to be tested to your business requirments that requires ATP or aATP during the Sales Quotation Process before the Sales Order/Solution Quotation/Solution Order is been triggerred

Once CPQ creates a quote and the user triggers an ATP check (either directly or when converting the quote to an order), S/4HANA performs:

  • Real-time availability check
  • Temporary quantity assignment (TQA)
  • Confirmation simulation
    These ATP checks help prevent false confirmations and maintain accuracy in early phase quotations.

3.2 ATP Simulation in the CPQ Process

Sales Order supports ATP simulation via OData APIs, allowing CPQ to request real-time availability before final order creation. 

3.3 Variant Configuration & ATP

When quotes involve configurable products, the integration leverages SAP Variant Configuration and Pricing, ensuring ATP checks consider(This is not part of the Custom Iflow Development):

  • Component availability
  • Alternative sourcing options
  • Production and supply constraints

4. Benefits of Integrating ATP/aATP with SAP CPQ

4.1 Accurate, Real-Time Quotes

aATP delivers real-time confirmations using S/4HANA’s in-memory performance, ensuring CPQ quotes reflect live supply chain data.

4.2 Reduced Order Rejections

By validating availability early, CPQ avoids orders failing later due to material shortages or unrealistic dates.

4.3 Improved Customer Commitment Accuracy

With ABC, BOP, and PAL, CPQ can provide delivery promises aligned with supply chain realities, reducing missed delivery windows.

4.5 Enhanced Sales Productivity

Sales reps can generate quotes more confidently without waiting for manual supply chain consultations.
SAP CPQ already accelerates complex quote creation, and ATP enhances accuracy

4.4 Optimized Fulfillment Decisions

aATP can automatically evaluate multiple warehouses, alternative plants, or substitute products before CPQ confirms availability.

Conclusion

SAP ATP/aATP transforms SAP CPQ from a powerful quoting tool into an end-to-end, availability-aware commitment system.
By combining CPQ’s configuration and pricing intelligence with real-time supply checks, organizations achieve:

  • Faster, more accurate quotations
  • Stronger order reliability
  • Greater customer satisfaction
  • Optimized supply chain execution

In a world where customers expect instant, accurate promises, integrating ATP/aATP with SAP CPQ is no longer optional—it’s a competitive necessity.

entry.title: Re: Managing unconfirmed receivables/payables (before inv...
entry.content:
Hello @byd 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, the public cloud supports cash flow forecasting before invoice posting by leverages operational exposures (for example from sales and PO) in One Exposure from Operations, which is used for cash and liquidity analysis. See the following reference for more insights: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/abd32554225fea6fe10000000a423f68.html?locale=en-US

However, it is important to align terminology:

  • POs and SOs do not carry payment schedules in the same way that posted Invoices do. Pre invoice cash forecast is typically based on planned operational flows stored in One Exposure from Operations with a lower certainty level.

Below are answers to your questions:

Question 1: Which Scope Item(s) support this functionality?

Answer: For Public Cloud cash and liquidity capabilities, there is a distinguishing between:

  • Basic cash management capability
  • Advanced cash scope that includes additional cash operations and bank account management features, that are delivered through scope items such as Advanced Bank Account Management and Advanced Cash Operations.

If your goal is operation forecasting and deeper cash operation tooling, you typically end up in the advanced cash scope territory. See KBA 3430899 for more information.

 Question 2: Which Fiori app(s) can be used to view/manage these unconfirmed payment schedules?

Answer: If your aim is to view and analyse pre invoice cash impacts, then use Cash Flow Analyser.

For managing the underlying operational dates, you manage them in their source documents:

  • Sales Orders
  • Purchase Orders

The cash layer itself is driven by One Exposure from Operations.

See the following reference for more details: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6b39bd1d0e5e4099a5b65d835c29c696/b54be33ef65c45a6be2dbb67487e432e.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/abd32554225fea6fe10000000a423f68.html?locale=en-US

Question 3: Can these schedules be automatically integrated with Cash Management for liquidity forecasting?

Answer: Yes, that is the standard concept. Operational documents contribute to One Exposer from Operations, which supports cash and liquidity process and analytics. Cash management apps consume this exposure data for KPIs and forecasting views.

See the following reference for more details: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6b39bd1d0e5e4099a5b65d835c29c696/b54be33ef65c45a6be2dbb67487e432e.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/abd32554225fea6fe10000000a423f68.html?locale=en-US

Question 4: Which license is required

Answer: This depends on the capability level you need:

  • Basic Cash Management covers basic capabilities, but advanced features and apps can require additional scope items and licences.
  • The Advanced Cash Operations Scope item requires an additional S4HANA Cloud licence. See KBA 3430899 for more information.
  • The S4HANA Cloud for cash management is also positioned as product offering. See the following for more details. https://www.sap.com/products/financial-management/cash-management.html

On your question “Base Bundle vs Premium Bundle”: I would not assume a 1:1 mapping from “Basic Cash Management vs S4HANA Cloud for cash management” to “Base vs Premium bundle” without checking your contract, because some features require separate subscription licences.

To summarise:

  • Pre invoice forecasting is supported via One Exposure from Operations capturing operational exposures.
  • Use Cash Flow Analyzer to view and drill down into planned item for liquidity forecasting.
  • Advanced cash capabilities may require scope items like Advanced Cash Operations, which requires an additional licence.
  • Base vs Premium bundle mapping depends on your contract. You need to confirm with SAP.

 Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Lost in Transit: Why AI Needs Business Context
entry.content:
Every enterprise executive I talk to is worried about their readiness for AI. They have read stark reports about how many organizations struggle to move AI pilot projects into production. They worry that they don't have the right skills on board, or the strategic insight into how their business could change. But they rarely understand that their biggest barrier is not algorithms, computing power or training, but something rather more mundane: the data that will underlie every AI deployment.

A recent TDWI survey of 1,200 IT and business leaders found that only 34% report high trust in their organization's data capabilities. That's a worrying figure when you consider the ambitions resting on top of it. Organizations are pouring resources into generative AI, predictive analytics, and intelligent automation; yet the majority of their own leaders wouldn't vouch for the data feeding those systems.

The Context Problem

It's worth understanding clearly where this problem - this "trust gap" - arises. To be clear, enterprise data doesn't begin its life untrusted. Inside SAP's ERP ecosystem, a customer record carries rich meaning: payment terms, credit history, regional classification, relationships to contracts and deliveries. That meaning is the business context, and it is what makes data useful for reasoning rather than merely for counting.

The trouble starts when data gets extracted from its systems of origin. Traditional analytics architectures, such as data warehouses or data lakes, pull information out of operational systems, flatten it into tables, and in doing so, strip away the very relationships that gave it business context and meaning. A customer ID in a data lake is just a string: the semantic richness that connected it to supply chains, financial flows, and service histories has gone.

Many of the problems we face with data quality and data integration, not just for AI, can be traced back to this same structural loss of context.

For conventional reporting or business intelligence, the damage was tolerable, or at least manageable. Dashboards could still show last quarter's revenue by region. But AI is a different proposition entirely. Machine learning models need connected information with intact relationships: they need context. A demand forecasting model that can't see how procurement terms interact with suppliers or relate sales to customer payment behavior will produce forecasts that look mathematically plausible yet miss the dynamics that actually drive the business.

From Extraction to Fabric

What can be done? SAP's architectural response to this problem is the Business Data Fabric: a layer that preserves business meaning and semantics while making information accessible across functions and systems. Rather than extracting and flattening data, a fabric federates access to operational data in place, maintaining the contextual relationships that make information meaningful.

SAP's approach to this through the Business Data Cloud is worth examining closely, because it addresses the "trust gap" at its structural root. The platform today delivers over 500 managed data products: pre-built, vendor-maintained datasets that carry business context from their source systems intact. A "Customer" entity means the same thing whether it appears in a financial planning scenario, a supply chain analysis, or a workforce model. This consistency of meaning is maintained by design rather than reconstructed after the fact through data quality and data integration projects.

This matters in practical ways. Consider workforce planning. An HR team evaluating headcount against project plans needs information that spans finance (cost centers, budget allocations), operations (project timelines, resource requirements), and human capital (skills, availability, attrition risk). In a traditional architecture, assembling this view requires weeks, if not months, of data integration work; even then, the pipeline may be fragile and break whenever any source system changes. With managed data products sharing a common semantic layer, the cross-functional view exists as a constant capability rather than a custom build.

Why This Conversation Needs to Start Now

The shift from extraction-based analytics to fabric-based intelligence represents one of the more consequential architectural transitions in enterprise IT. And I say that one who spent years building data extraction and integration products.

It is also, frankly, an unglamorous transition. Nobody puts "semantic consistency of business entities" on a conference banner. But AI feels like the key subject of every event. But the organizations getting real value from those AI projects, the few moving past pilots into production, are precisely those that have solved this problem.

For leaders, the practical question is whether your current architecture can support the AI ambitions your board is funding. If your data strategy still depends on extracting information from operational systems and hoping that downstream processes can reconstruct the context, the answer is probably not yet.

The Window

Enterprise technology adoption follows familiar patterns. Organizations that build trusted data infrastructure early will compound their advantage as their AI capabilities develop and mature. Those who delay will find the gap harder to close, not because the technology becomes less available, but because organizational learning and data intelligence take time that can't be compressed.

The trust gap in enterprise data is real: indeed, it is measurable and consequential. Closing it requires deliberate, long-term architectural choices rather than tools layered on top of existing patterns. That conversation is worth having with those building data platforms and the practitioners who have already begun the journey.

If you’d like to explore these ideas further and learn how a data fabric preserves business context for AI at scale, join SAP and customer executives from leading companies live at The Fabric of Data & AI virtual event on March 26: https://url.sap/vlm80u

entry.title: Re: Issue in enabling custom attributes in UIBB for AD_PO...
entry.content:
Hi @Rajalakshmi_Sivamani ,

For Data Model extension topics, it's crucial to all related structures needed to contain your custom field. One of the important layers is GENIL when it comes to UI <-> Data Model connection.

SAP provides us structure points to add these custom fields with EEW. If I understand your scenario correctly, you need to add your custom field under BUS_EI_STRUC_ADDRESS structure (if you add your custom field in include BAPIBUS1006_ADDRESS, you also extended the related GENIL object's attribute structure so you can see your custom field in UI configuration).

Also, please do the other required steps like SMT mapping etc. for a successful Data Model extension.

entry.title: Re: GenAI Hub SDK, No deployment found with: deployment.m...
entry.content: I experienced the same error as well. After searching for related issues, I found that there were many updates to the LangChain library at the end of 2025. One of those changes was that langchain.prompts was replaced with langchain_core.prompts. I resolved the issue by changing "from langchain.prompts import PromptTemplate" to "from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate". If this solution doesn’t work, you could also try deleting your virtual environment and reinstalling the Python libraries, since the problem might be related to dependency conflicts. I hope my experience helps you too.

Що це дає:

  • Зручніше ознайомлення з важливою інформацією рідною мовою.
  • Швидша підготовка внутрішніх інструкцій та комунікацій для
  • Менше ризиків непорозумінь через мовні бар’єри.

Перевіряйте переклад у вашому середовищі та діліться враженнями! Будь які  Пропозиції щодо формулювань щиро вітаються — це допоможе зробити матеріал ще кориснішим для всієї спільноти.

entry.title: GRC Firefighter Log Review
entry.content:
Dear Friends,
In SAP GRC Access Control 12.0, Access Request (ARM) already pulls Manager from Active Directory (AD) via LDAP (connector) and shows it in User Details → Org Data → Manager.

For Emergency Access Management (EAM), we want Firefighter Log Review work items to go to the user’s Manager (same source/logic as ARM), instead of the Firefighter Controller.

Questions:
1. What are the supported options to determine the approver for the Firefighter Log Review workflow (Process: SAP_GRAC_FIREFIGHT_LOG_REPORT)— e.g., GRC API, BRF+ FM Call, LDAP attribute etc. 

2. If reusing AD’s manager attribute is recommended, what’s the cleanest way to implement?
e.g., MSMP Agent Rule (BRFplus Rule) calling GRC LDAP APIs, parameter mapping tips, pitfalls, and performance considerations.

3. Are there any standard agents or notes that support Manager‑based routing for EAM logs (instead of GRAC_SPM_CNTRL_AGENT)?

Landscape/Context:
GRC AC 12.0, EAM de-centralized
AD/LDAP connector (User Search/Details/Auth data sources)
ARM Manager is correct and comes from AD (we want the same for EAM)

Thanks
Raj

entry.title: On-Stack Partner Reference App: A Guide to Clean-Core Extensions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
As SAP partners and customers continue to innovate in the SAP cloud ERP space, one principle remains non-negotiable: keep the core clean. While SAP’s extensibility guidelines often favor side-by-side builds on the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), some scenarios require tighter, real-time integration with core processes. For these tightly coupled cases, on-stack extensibility within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides the essential solution. It offers a powerful way to enhance standard business processes—without modifying SAP-delivered code—enabling end users to innovate securely and at scale.

To bridge theory and practice, the On-Stack Partner Reference Application provides a practical blueprint for building these clean-core extensions. Using modern ABAP and RAP principles, it demonstrates how to extend standard SAP applications in a secure, upgrade-stable, and scalable way, fully aligned with SAP guidelines.

 When to Choose On-Stack Extensibility

While a side-by-side architecture on SAP Business Technology Platform is a powerful approach, it isn’t the ideal fit for every business need. Sometimes, an extension must be tightly woven into the main system to deliver the best results. On-stack extensibility is the recommended path for these tightly coupled scenarios, especially when your solution requires:

  • Deep integration with standard business processes
  • Seamless navigation across SAP Fiori applications
  • A harmonized user experience
  • Enhancements that are upgrade-safe within the same system

On-stack extensibility allows developers and organizations to achieve these goals by building directly within the SAP S/4HANA Cloud stack. This approach preserves the clean-core architecture, ensuring the system remains stable and maintainable. The Partner Reference Application demonstrates the recommended “golden path” for achieving this perfect balance.

The Reference Scenario: Loyalty Hub

When on-stack extensibility is the right approach, the On-Stack Partner Reference Application provides a hands-on example that demonstrates how to extend standard SAP processes securely and efficiently. It goes beyond theory to present a full end-to-end scenario that partners can explore, adapt, and use as a practical blueprint.

The scenario uses a Loyalty Hub application integrated with the standard Manage Sales Orders – Version 2 SAP Fiori application. Its goal is to help organizations manage customer loyalty programs directly within sales operations, while preserving seamless navigation between standard SAP functionality and the custom extension.

To make the scenario concrete, we demonstrate the application using two personas:

  • Aarti, the loyalty administrator, manages memberships, awards loyalty points, and issues gift cards
  • Julia, the sold-to party or customer, earns points, redeems rewards, and applies gift cards during sales transactions

This approach highlights both administrative and customer perspectives, showing how Loyalty Hub supports real-world workflows and interactions. Here’s how the Loyalty Hub works from both the administrative and customer perspectives:

Key Capabilities

The application delivers comprehensive loyalty management:

  • Handles memberships, tiers, and related transactions
  • Tracks and awards loyalty points automatically in response to sales order events
  • Issues and redeems gift cards tied to customer orders
  • Sends automated notifications for points updates and tier changes
  • Supports both administrator workflows and end-user interactions

Technical and Operational Design

  • Built with ABAP RAP and custom business objects in the Z namespace
  • Data modeled using CDS, ensuring separation from standard SAP objects and upgrade stability
  • User interfaces designed with SAP Fiori elements, supporting flexible extensions and semantic navigation
  • SAP-standard authentication and role-based authorization for secure access
  • Operational readiness includes scheduled jobs for membership evaluation, number range handling for controlled object creation, and application logging/messaging for monitoring and troubleshooting

This scenario illustrates how partners can innovate within the SAP S/4HANA Cloud stack, extending functionality while preserving clean-core principles, upgrade safety, and a consistent SAP user experience — all while reflecting both administrative and customer perspectives.

Key Business Value

The reference application helps your organization accelerate development while staying fully compliant with clean-core principles. By using it as a guide, your teams can:

  • Gain a reference architecture aligned with SAP standards.
  • Use proven patterns to integrate with standard applications.
  • Learn to build reusable and deployable solutions.
  • Accelerate the delivery of robust and upgrade-safe extensions.

The Loyalty Hub demonstrates that innovation and compliance can coexist—adding real business value without compromising the SAP clean core. The best way to understand the power of this approach is to see it in action.

You can get started and try the Loyalty Hub example by accessing the complete reference application on GitHub. By following this framework, you can confidently deliver the innovative, compliant, and upgrade-safe solutions your business requires.

On Stack Partner Reference Extension for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Explore Other Reference Applications

Depending on your extensibility strategy, you can also explore:

  • Partner Reference Application for SAP BTP ABAP , Blog
  • Cross-Stack Partner Reference Extension
  • Partner Reference Application for SAP BTP and CAP

Together, these resources guide developers and architects across on-stack, side-by-side, and cross-stack extension patterns—helping them choose the right architecture while staying aligned with SAP’s clean-core and cloud-first principles.

entry.title: Re: SAP IQ roadmap
entry.content:
SAP IQ continues to be fully supported with regular ongoing releases including the SAP IQ 16.2 release and subsequent PL release in 2025. Regular PL releases will continue to be delivered. 

New features related to SAP IQ are being delivered through SAP HANA Cloud, Data Lake Relational Engine which is the cloud native version of SAP IQ. There are no significant new features planned for on premise SAP IQ.

entry.title: Re: Pricing error: Mandatory condition TTX1 is missing
entry.content:
Dear @Sarah_Peart,

What the error actually means is in pricing analysis, SAP tried to read condition records for TTX1 and found none

What to do in Public Cloud:

1. In the sales order open Pricing Analysis ->condition TTX1 ->Access details and note the exact key SAP is searching with especially Departure Country/Region and Destination

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002799312

2. Maintain the missing record in Set/Manage Tax Rates Sales (App F6972) for that exact combination for example Departure SE, Destination GB, classifications, etc.

More details below:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F6972')/S35

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003646274

3. If the needed tax code / % doesn’t exist yet, create/adjust it in CBC activity 101016 Define Tax Codes for Sales and Purchases (incl. valid-from and tax percentage rate)

 

4. Reprice the sales order

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: 1.在维护凭证打印科目凭证输出的过程中，无法维护新增为公司代码（日本公司）激活凭证编号，无法分配编号组到公...
entry.content:
Good day @lemon-xue 

Thank you for your question.

The IDCN_ACC_DOC_OM template and the app Accounts Document Output China belong to China localization and rely on China voucher printing and ODN relate configuration. See the following KBA 2871884 and Blog: Understanding the "Account Document Output" App and Customizing Form Templates for more insights.

ODN and voucher numbering are dependent on the designated country. If your company code is Japan, you cannot activate China voucher numbering for it, so you may not be able to maintain the “activate voucher number for company code” entry and voucher number can be empty in the China output app. This Blog provides some details: Official document numbering (ODN) in SAP S/4 HANA Cloud

If the company code is actually China, then the issue is a missing ODN activation or assignment step. In that case, you should verify ODN activation and number group assignments in CBC for CN, then retest the voucher output.

The following reference provides guidance: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a624a4a6d1d8473eb95fb42660127cfe/5de921d15390426ea9b574c57dc990b8.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Data Replication from GTS to SAP Business data cloud
entry.content:
Hello All,

We have an existing SAP Global Trade Services system and are planning to integrate it with SAP Business Data Cloud.

Our objective is to bring GTS data into SAP Datasphere, combine it with other enterprise and external datasets (such as weather and cost indices), and leverage Databricks for predictive analytics and forecasting.

Could you please clarify:

  • What is the recommended data movement architecture from GTS to Datasphere and further into Databricks for predictive use cases? We came across Hana data lake files which can be embedded into Datasphere and we can move data in parquet or csv format which can act as source to Databricks.
  • Whether data needs to be physically replicated into Databricks or can be accessed virtually?
  • How data storage is handled across Datasphere (BDC) and Databricks, and whether this results in data duplication?
  • What are the key cost components involved, specifically for:
    • BDC/Datasphere storage and processing (CU-based)
    • Databricks storage and compute (DBU-based)
    • Data movement or integration costs
    • What are the best practices to optimize storage and compute costs for such predictive scenarios?

      Any help would be appreciated

entry.title: Re: Can we call a remote API to update a business object in S4 using a custom entity in BTP ABAP?
entry.content:
Yes, might be with strict(1), but as we need extensibility, which requires strict(2), this is not possible in the save (modified) currently. In the interaction phase this is always possible. But with concurrent local and remote modification you would need 2 phase commit to make everything consistent in all events.

And yes, I agree with you, this could be documented better.

entry.title: SAP BTP – GDCR/DDCR - Domain-Driven-Centric-Router on APIM & CPI (1.49M Validated Requests)
entry.content:
Introduction

Enterprise API landscapes are traditionally organized around vendors and backend systems.
However, business processes are domain-centric by nature.

Gateway Domain-Centric Routing (GDCR) proposes a vendor-agnostic architectural model where APIs are exposed per business domain and backend resolution is handled dynamically through deterministic, metadata-driven routing.

The implementation repository referenced in the DOI contains artifacts specifically focused on the SAP BTP Integration Suite, particularly:

  • SAP API Management (referred to as DCRP)
  • SAP Cloud Integration (referred to as PDCP)

At the SAP CPI level, the “domain-centric” alignment applies strictly to structural naming conventions and governance organization — including:

  • Technical users
  • Packages
  • iFlow DNA structure
  • Value Mapping entries
  • Script collections (global or per domain)

It does not introduce additional complexity to iFlow development itself. If interpreted as an attempt to increase development complexity, the proposal is being misunderstood.

The CPI layer remains technically conventional; the innovation lies in semantic organization and metadata-driven routing governance — not in altering integration development fundamentals.

At the SAP API Management level, the model is more sophisticated and requires practical APIM experience. However, after reading the DOI and understanding the deterministic routing lifecycle, the provided documentation and artifacts should enable an architect with API Management background to build a working Proof of Concept in approximately 30 minutes.

The repository provides structural implementation support.
The DOI provides the architectural reasoning required to understand and reproduce the model correctly.

A Vendor-Agnostic Metadata-Driven Architecture for Enterprise API Governance

Enterprise API landscapes are typically organized around systems and vendors

  • Salesforce APIs
  • SAP APIs
  • AWS APIs
  • Azure APIs

However, business processes are not vendor-centric. They are domain-centric:

  • Order-to-Cash
  • Procure-to-Pay
  • Record-to-Report

Gateway Domain-Centric Routing (GDCR) proposes a different model:

Framework Components

  • GDCR – Gateway Domain-Centric Routing
  • DDCR – Deterministic routing engine
  • PDCP – Package Domain-Centric Pattern (SAP CPI alignment name conventions)
  • DCRP –  Domain Centric-Routing Pattern ( SAP APIM )

Expose one proxy per business domain, and resolve backend systems dynamically through semantic metadata.

No proprietary, confidential, or actual production customer data was ever accessed or used at any time during the research efforts described in this work. All validations were executed on trial/sandbox environments.

Core Concept

GDCR separates:

  • Business intent (domain / entity / action)
  • Technical execution (endpoint / adapter / protocol)

Inbound URLs such as:

/sales/orders/create/salesforce

are normalized into canonical routing keys, validated against a metadata control plane (KVM), and dynamically bound to backend endpoints.mProxies remain immutable.mBackend systems can change without impacting consumers.

The DDCR Engine

At the heart of the framework is the Domain-Driven Centric Router (DDCR) — a deterministic seven-stage lifecycle:

  • Parse
  • Normalize
  • Canonicalize
  • Validate (Route Guard)
  • Lookup (Metadata resolution)
  • Bind (Dynamic target resolution)
  • Execute

Security is enforced through:

  • Canonical normalization of action space
  • Whitelist validation before lookup
  • Fail-fast rejection (HTTP 500) for unregistered combinations
  • Backend URLs never derived from client input

The engine operates as a Layer-7 semantic control plane, analogous to control/data plane separation in SDN and service mesh architectures.

Novelty and positioning

DDCR does not introduce isolated new primitives.
Instead, it formalizes a deterministic composition of established integration principles into a prescriptive execution model for enterprise API governance.

The contribution lies in defining a runtime-executable semantic routing grammar with a fixed seven-stage lifecycle invariant, independent of vendor platform.

This lifecycle binds:

  • Canonical domain-semantic normalization
  • Explicit whitelist validation (Route Guard)
  • Metadata-driven dynamic backend resolution
  • Immutable gateway façades

  • into a single deterministic execution chain.

While metadata-driven routing, domain-oriented API structuring, and control/data-plane separation concepts exist in prior integration paradigms, GDCR/DDCR articulates them as a unified Layer-7 Semantic Control Plane with strict lifecycle guarantees and fail-fast safety constraints.

The innovation is therefore architectural formalization and deterministic execution invariance — not individual technical mechanisms in isolation.By separating business intent (domain / entity / action) from technical execution (endpoint / adapter / protocol), the framework enables vendor interchangeability, backend evolution, and governance stability without consumer impact.

— Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana March 02, 2026

Cross-Platform Validation (February 2026)

The model was validated across:

  • SAP BTP (API Management + Cloud Integration)
  • Kong (Docker)
  • AWS + Cloud Integration
  • Microsoft Azure + Cloud Integration

Total execution:

  • 1,499,869 requests
  • 100% routing success (zero routing failures)
  • 99.9916% end-to-end success
  • All failures classified as trial/sandbox network interruptions (ECONNRESET / ETIMEDOUT)

All validation was performed exclusively in trial/sandbox environments. No production or confidential data was accessed.

Newman JavaScript Policy - Phantom v12:

On SAP BTP specifically, the DDCR Phantom v12 engine processed 121,471 requests through API Management and Cloud Integration (4 domains, 44 vendors, 4 proxies, 44 iFlows) with 99.9967% success and a cross‑Atlantic latency of

p50 145 ms, p85 184 ms, p99 338 ms — all with immutable proxies and routing policies.

Architectural Trade-Off: Determinism vs Encoding

Routing metadata is intentionally human-readable:

gdcrpordersusalesforceemeaid02:http

A strict O(1) binary encoding could increase lookup speed marginally, but at the cost of operational transparency and auditability.

Current routing resolution averages ~2 ms while preserving:

          • Human inspection
          • Zero external tooling dependency
          • Deterministic fail-fast behavior

After reviewing the DOI documentation and deploying the provided artifacts, architects with API Management experience should be able to execute an initial PoC within roughly 10–30 minutes.

 

Mandatory Documentation & Implementation Guidance

Full formal documentation is available at:

DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18582492

Downloading and reading the DOI document is mandatory before attempting any implementation.

The document contains:

  • 12 pages of architectural explanation
  • 15 pages of complementary diagrams, routing matrices, and structural tables

The GitHub repository is referenced inside the DOI document.

Accessing the repository without first reading the DOI will have little practical value and may lead to misinterpretation.

Github - In the folder:

/gdcr-proven/gdcr-dcrp-sap-api/files-repository

You will find:
- 4 SAP APIM proxies
- 44 static routing mappings
- 10 business processes structured by domain
- 4 CPI packages
- 44 iFlows

To execute the Postman collection:

  • Replace the host
  • Replace username and password credentials

Important

The Key Value Mapping (KVM) is not exportable and must be created manually. It is strongly recommended to keep the provided naming conventions.nIf names are changed, the policy logic must be adjusted accordingly.

Example KVM:

cpipackage-nx.sales.o2c.integrations | cpipackage-nx.finance.r2r.integrations


GDCR is an architectural framework — not a configuration shortcut.

Note:

The internal routing references used during validation were environment-specific and will not function externally without proper configuration.

Due to platform constraints, certain Key-Value Mapping (KVM) strings were not exportable.

The deterministic routing grammar and metadata construction logic required to rebuild the KVM entries are fully explained in the DOI.

Without understanding:

  • Domain normalization logic
  • Routing identifier construction
  • Metadata abstraction model
  • Deterministic resolution sequence

it is not possible to correctly reconstruct the routing configuration.

The repository provides structure.


The DOI provides the logic.

The DOI must be read first.

License, Attribution & Publication Record

© 2026 Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana

This publication establishes a public prior publication record of the GDCR/DDCR framework as of February 7, 2026 (initial DOI release).

This work is released under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) License.

You are free to use, copy, reproduce, modify, distribute, and build upon this framework for any purpose, including commercial applications, provided that appropriate attribution is given to the original author and reference to the DOI is maintained.

Full formal documentation:

DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18582492

🛡️ Semantic Architecture. Deterministic Governance. Vendor Independence.

Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana - March 02, 2026

Enterprise Integration Architect
SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert all capabilities
SAP Press e-Bite Author — Enterprise Messaging (2021)

#GDCR #DDCR #EnterpriseArchitecture #APIGovernance #SAPBTPIntegrationSuite #SAPAPIM #SAPCPI #OpenSource #IntegrationPatterns #VendorAgnostic #Microservices

entry.title: Re: Custom Cube for Margin Analysis - Calculated Key Figure shows no data
entry.content:
Hello Koushik,

thanks for the info. I was able to fix my issue with the aggregation. I didn´t know the difference between OLAP aggregation (which i used in the consumption view) and SQL aggregation (which i have to use here in the cube). I tried to aggregate data with OLAP annotation and that does not work in the cube.

The raw measure cube - no aggregation

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCO_MA_ST_RM'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'MarginAna DF Raw Measure CUBE Part 2'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
@VDM.viewType: #COMPOSITE
@Analytics.dataCategory: #CUBE
@Analytics.internalName: #LOCAL
@Search.searchable: false
@ObjectModel.usageType.sizeCategory: #XXL
@ObjectModel.usageType.dataClass:  #MIXED
@ObjectModel.usageType.serviceQuality: #D


define view ZCO_I_MA_ST_DF_RM_CUBE
with parameters
    P_GLAccountHierarchy : fins_sem_tag_hryid,
    P_CompanyCode        : bukrs

as select from I_MargAnlysProdAndSrvcCube
    ( P_GLAccountHierarchy : $parameters.P_GLAccountHierarchy )

{

//// Dimensions ////

  key cast( Ledger      as fins_ledger ) as Ledger,
  key cast( CompanyCode as bukrs       ) as CompanyCode,
  key cast( FiscalYear  as fis_gjahr   ) as FiscalYear,
  key FiscalYearPeriod,
  key GLAccountHierarchy,
  key SemanticTag,




//// Raw Units of Measure ////

  cast( BaseUnit            as meins        ) as BaseUnit,
  cast( CompanyCodeCurrency as fis_hwaer    ) as CompanyCodeCurrency,
  cast( GlobalCurrency      as fis_rkcur    ) as GlobalCurrency,


//// Restricted Measures ////

  @EndUserText.label: 'Revenue'
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'GlobalCurrency'
  case when SemanticTag = 'ZGS_C_PRIC' and Ledger = '0L'
    then AmountInGlobalCurrency
    else 0 end as RevenueGlobalCurr,

  @EndUserText.label: 'COGS'
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'GlobalCurrency'
  case when SemanticTag = 'ZGS_L_PRIC' and Ledger = '0L'
    then AmountInGlobalCurrency
    else 0 end as COGSGlobalCurr,

  @EndUserText.label: 'FIDirect'
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'GlobalCurrency'
  case when SemanticTag = 'ZGS_DISCLP' and Ledger = '0L'
    then AmountInGlobalCurrency
    else 0 end as FIDirectGlobalCurr,

  @EndUserText.label: 'OverhAlloc'
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'GlobalCurrency'
  case when SemanticTag = 'ZGS_PR_SUR' and Ledger = '0L'
    then AmountInGlobalCurrency
    else 0 end as OverheadAllocGlobalCurr

//  _SemanticTag

}
where CompanyCode   = $parameters.P_CompanyCode
  and Ledger        = '0L'

On top a Cube for aggregation

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCO_MA_ST_AGG'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'MarginAna DF Raw Measure CUBE AGG Part 2'
@VDM.viewType: #COMPOSITE
@Analytics.dataCategory: #CUBE


define view ZCO_I_MA_ST_DF_RM_AGG
  with parameters
    P_GLAccountHierarchy : fins_sem_tag_hryid,
    P_CompanyCode        : bukrs
    
    
as select from ZCO_I_MA_ST_DF_RM_CUBE(
    P_GLAccountHierarchy : $parameters.P_GLAccountHierarchy,
    P_CompanyCode        : $parameters.P_CompanyCode
)

{
  key Ledger,
  key CompanyCode,
  key FiscalYear,
  key FiscalYearPeriod,
  key GLAccountHierarchy,
  GlobalCurrency,

// In classic CDS (SQL semantics), the moment you use an aggregate function like sum( ), 
// the SELECT becomes an aggregating SELECT. Then the rule is:  every element that is not aggregated must be listed in a GROUP BY.

  @EndUserText.label: 'Revenue'
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'GlobalCurrency'
  sum( RevenueGlobalCurr )       as RevenueGlobalCurr,

  @EndUserText.label: 'COGS'
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'GlobalCurrency'
  sum( COGSGlobalCurr )          as COGSGlobalCurr,
  
  @EndUserText.label: 'Revenue + COGS'
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'GlobalCurrency'
  sum( RevenueGlobalCurr ) + sum( COGSGlobalCurr ) as RevenuePlusCOGSGlobalCurr,
  

  @EndUserText.label: 'FIDirect'
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'GlobalCurrency'
  sum( FIDirectGlobalCurr )      as FIDirectGlobalCurr,

  @EndUserText.label: 'OverhAlloc'
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'GlobalCurrency'
  sum( OverheadAllocGlobalCurr ) as OverheadAllocGlobalCurr
  
  
}
group by
  Ledger,
  CompanyCode,
  FiscalYear,
  FiscalYearPeriod,
  GLAccountHierarchy,
  GlobalCurrency

  And the result is that 58 rows were compressed/aggregated to 5 rows. 5 rows is correct because of FISCALYEARPERIOD.

Using SUM and GROUP BY was the solution.

Happy with the result 🙂

entry.title: Re: Discount on invoices
entry.content:
1. Why you see KZBTR in BSEG but not in ACDOCA
In BSEG, the field KZBTR (Cash Discount Amount) is explicitly stored for line items where payment terms allow discounts. In ACDOCA, the Universal Journal does not carry a direct equivalent field. Instead, discounts are reflected through posting logic and condition types, not as a standalone field.

2. Where to look in ACDOCA
Discounts are not stored as a separate field in ACDOCA. Instead they appear as separate line items (with their own posting key and account assignment) when the discount is realized.

You can identify them by checking:

  • ACDOCA-HKONT (G/L account) → the discount clearing or expense account.
  • ACDOCA-BSTAT / ACDOCA-BUZEI → line item indicators.
  • ACDOCA-REBZT (if rebate/discount relevant).

The amount field is always ACDOCA-TSL / ACDOCA-HSL (transaction/local currency).

3. Standard SAP Behavior
Yes, this is standard. ACDOCA is designed to be a harmonized journal. Instead of duplicating fields like KZBTR, it represents discounts as separate postings. This makes reporting more flexible but requires you to identify discounts by account or document type rather than a single field.

4. Discounts at Invoice vs. Payment

At invoice posting: No discount is posted yet — only terms are stored in BKPF/BSEG.
At payment clearing: The discount is realized and posted as a separate line item in ACDOCA.

That’s why you won’t see KZBTR in ACDOCA at invoice stage, but you will see a discount line item once payment is made with discount taken.

Currently, my CAP app uses 2 separate instances of the standalone Event Mesh. I had to adopt this architecture because of the high number of Cloud Integration iFlows involved: I exceeded the hard limit of 200 concurrent connections per instance (which are established during the deployment of the iFlows using the AMQP adapter).

My questions regarding the new integrated Event Mesh in SAP Integration Suite are:

  1. Are 400 connections still supported overall in this new integrated version?

  2. Is it still possible (or necessary) to configure multiple instances/message clients to handle this volume, or have the connection limits changed?

  3. If my current approach (splitting across 2 instances) is no longer supported, what is the recommended best practice or architectural approach to migrate this specific scenario?

Thank you very much for your support.

Best regards,

Andrea

entry.title: Re: Upload rebate credit memo to KSEF (Polish National Sy...
entry.content:
ECC rebate settlement does not natively support invoice references in credit memos. KSeF requires them. The practical solution is to enhance the KSeF interface to pull invoice references from rebate index tables and enrich the XML. This is standard practice in Polish ECC/S4 implementations.

Having said that, the Best Practice is keep ECC rebate settlement as-is. Build a KSeF interface enhancement that pulls invoice references from rebate index tables and enriches the XML. This avoids disrupting the rebate process while meeting KSeF’s legal requirement.

entry.content: Via Launchpad Notification (resp a custom build similar function which we developed before Launchpad Notificaton were usable), because in this way the message will be available to the user even if the application is left before background processing is finished.

entry.title: Re: Budget exceeded error BP603, WBS element - PS Module
entry.content:
Standard Logic Behind Availability Control

  • Budget availability control (AVC) in PS is always checked at the project/WBS hierarchy level defined in customizing (e.g., overall project, WBS element, or statistical key figure).
  • When you allocate costs from one WBS to another within the same project, the system tries to validate against the budget profile of the receiving WBS element.
  • If the allocation standard points to another WBS in the same project, the system does not “roll up” to the project budget — it checks only the local WBS element budget.
  • That’s why you see the exceeded budget error equal to the allocation amount: the system isn’t retrieving the overall project balance, only the local WBS element’s budget.

Why It Worked Across Projects

  • When you tested with a WBS element from another project and disabled AVC (CJBW), the system didn’t enforce the same intra-project budget check.
  • Cross-project allocations are treated differently: the sending project’s AVC doesn’t apply to the receiving project’s WBS element, so the error disappears.

Is This Standard SAP Behavior?

  • Yes. SAP standard does not allow one WBS element to use another WBS element from the same project as its “budget source” in allocation standards.
  • The design assumption is that budget availability is controlled at the project or WBS level, not by cross-referencing within the same hierarchy.

Options / Workarounds

1) Change AVC Profile Level
In customizing (SPRO → Project System → Costs → Budget → Define Budget Profile), set availability control to the overall project level instead of WBS element level. This way, allocations between WBS elements within the same project will check against the total project budget, not just the receiving WBS.

2) Use Statistical WBS Elements
If you want one WBS to act as a “budget pool” for others, define it as a statistical WBS element and assign budget there. Then configure AVC to check at the project level.

3) Custom Enhancement
Some projects implement a user exit (e.g., EXIT_SAPLCJWB_001) to override how the budget is checked, so that intra-project allocations can reference another WBS’s budget. This is not standard and requires careful design to avoid audit issues.

entry.title: Service Order Template Migration – Missing Fields (Product ID / Template Search Term) and Empty API
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

I am currently migrating Service Order Templates in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and need to set the following attributes during migration:

  • Status

  • Product ID

  • Template Search Term

The migration object does not expose the fields Product ID and Template Search Term, so they cannot be maintained via the standard migration template.

As an alternative, I attempted to update these fields using the OData service API_SERVICE_ORDER_TEMPLATE_SRV. However, the API " GET" call returns an empty response, although Service Order Templates are clearly available in the system (verified in the UI).

I would appreciate clarification on the following points:

  1. Is there a supported way to populate Product ID and Template Search Term during migration?

  2. Are these fields technically supported for update via API?

  3. Under which conditions would API_SERVICE_ORDER_TEMPLATE_SRV return something

System context: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud (3-system landscape, Migration Cockpit – “Migrate Your Data” app).

Best regards 

Simon

entry.title: Re: Custom logic for Payment media file
entry.content:
Hello@Dinakar1996__19

You seem to have chosen the wrong tag (Public Cloud) while posting your question.

The scenario you described is a classic payment medium exit logic (a Private Cloud/On-prem solution), which is not the supported extensibility approach in Public Cloud.

If your goal is still to “split a 60 char value into 35 + 25 in the payment file”, the public cloud offers options such as:

  • Payment format configuration (no ABAP),
  • Use a second output field that the bank format supports and,
  • Adjust the upstream data model (where you maintain the supplier or customer address in two street lines so you do not need runtime splitting).

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Migrating to SAP HANA Cloud: What Actually Gets Better (Part 1 of 2)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY |  TECHNICAL GUIDE  |  SAP HANA CLOUD |  PART 1 OF 2

Migrating to SAP HANA Cloud: What Actually Gets Better

Part 1 of 2: The genuine capabilities that open up when you move from on-premise HANA to HANA Cloud — HDI containers, SAP AI Core, and the BTP application ecosystem.

By Deepa Dorairaj   | SAP AI Solution Architect  |  HANA Data Engineering  |  Published 2026

This is Part 1 of a two-part series on SAP HANA Cloud migration. Part 1 covers what genuinely improves when you move to the cloud. Part 2 covers the pain points and what to watch out for. Both perspectives are necessary for an honest picture of the migration journey.

TL;DR: Moving from on-premise HANA to HANA Cloud is not just an infrastructure change. It unlocks three capabilities that are difficult or impossible to replicate on-premise: a modern database development workflow through HDI containers and Git, access to SAP AI Core for production-grade AI deployments on your SAP data, and a rich application ecosystem through BTP that connects your HANA data to S/4HANA, Fiori, Integration Suite, and beyond.

Why This Article Exists

Most migration content falls into one of two traps: vendor-produced material that glosses over the real challenges, or war stories that focus exclusively on what broke. The honest picture is more nuanced than either.

Part 2 of this series covers the friction — the tooling gaps, scripting compatibility issues, and security model changes that will slow your team down. You should read that too, especially if you are in the planning phase.

But this article is about the other side of that equation: what genuinely gets better. Not what SAP's marketing says gets better — what actually changes for the teams doing real work in real HANA Cloud environments. The three areas covered here represent capabilities that were either unavailable, severely limited, or operationally painful in on-premise. In HANA Cloud they become first-class, production-ready patterns.

IMPROVEMENT 1 OF 3 

HDI Containers: A Modern Database Development Workflow

What HDI Actually Is — and Why It Changes Everything

The HANA Deployment Infrastructure (HDI) container model is the single biggest workflow improvement that HANA Cloud brings for development teams. On-premise HANA, most teams deploy database objects directly against schemas — DDL scripts run manually or through transport management, objects exist in a shared schema that everyone touches, and version control is an afterthought bolted on top of an inherently stateful system.

HDI flips this model entirely. Database objects are defined as source artifacts — .hdbtable, .hdbview, .hdbprocedure files — that live in a Git repository alongside application code. The HDI container is deployed from those artifacts, and the deployment engine handles dependency resolution, delta deployment, and environment isolation automatically.

The result is that your database development workflow starts to look like your application development workflow: version controlled, reviewable, deployable, and reproducible.

Git Integration: Version Control as a First-Class Citizen

On-premise HANA development rarely had true version control for database objects. Teams used SAP Transport Management for moving objects between landscapes, but transport management is not version control — it doesn't give you branching, pull requests, history, or the ability to roll back to any point in time with confidence.

HANA Cloud's HDI model integrates directly with Git repositories through SAP Business Application Studio and the HANA Cloud toolchain. Every database object is a file in a repository. Changes go through the same review process as application code. Deployments are triggered from specific commits or branches.

  • Feature branches for database changes — developers work on isolated branches without affecting the shared schema, exactly as they would for application code.
  • Pull request reviews for schema changes — DDL changes are reviewed by peers before deployment, catching errors before they reach development or production environments.
  • Full commit history for every database object — the ability to see exactly what changed, when, and who made the change, with the ability to revert to any previous state.
  • Branch-based environment promotion — development, QA, and production environments are deployed from specific branches, making the promotion process auditable and repeatable.

This is the change that development teams feel most immediately after migration. The frustration of untraceable schema changes, overwritten objects, and transport conflicts disappears when database artifacts live in Git like everything else.

Environment Isolation and Team Collaboration

On-premise HANA landscapes typically have a development system, a QA system, and a production system — three full HANA instances that are expensive to maintain and still don't provide true isolation between individual developers working simultaneously.

HDI containers provide lightweight, isolated development environments within a single HANA Cloud instance. Each developer or team can have their own container — a complete, isolated copy of the database schema — without requiring a separate HANA system. Changes in one container have zero impact on another.

  • Developer containers — each developer works in their own isolated container, testing changes without risk of interfering with colleagues.
  • Feature containers — a complete isolated environment for a specific feature or project, spun up and torn down as needed.
  • Automated dependency management — the HDI deployment engine resolves object dependencies automatically. If a view depends on a table that doesn't exist yet, the deployment fails with a clear error rather than creating an inconsistent state silently.
  • Simplified landscape transport — promoting changes from development to QA to production means deploying the same Git artifacts to different containers, not managing SAP transport requests.

Cleaner Deployment and Versioning

One of the most underappreciated HDI benefits is idempotent deployment. On-premise, re-running a DDL script against an existing object requires DROP and recreate logic, ALTER statements, or manual intervention. HDI's deployment engine compares the desired state (your artifact files) against the current state (what's in the container) and applies only the necessary changes — automatically, safely, and repeatably.

-- On-premise: manual drop and recreate required

DROP TABLE my_schema.my_table;

CREATE TABLE my_schema.my_table (id INT, name VARCHAR(100));

-- HDI: define once in .hdbtable artifact, deploy repeatedly

-- HDI engine handles delta — no manual DROP/CREATE needed

COLUMN TABLE my_table (id INT, name NVARCHAR(100));

 

Capability

What It Unlocks

Git version control

Full history, branching, pull request reviews, and rollback for every database object

Developer isolation

Individual HDI containers eliminate shared schema conflicts between developers

Automated dependency resolution

HDI deployment engine manages object dependencies — no manual sequencing of DDL scripts

Idempotent deployment

Deploy the same artifacts repeatedly without manual DROP/CREATE logic

Landscape transport

Git-based promotion replaces SAP transport requests for database objects

Feature branching

Database changes developed and tested in isolation before merging to main branch

IMPROVEMENT 2 OF 3 

SAP AI Core: Production-Grade AI on Your SAP Data

Why AI Core Changes the Enterprise AI Equation

On-premise HANA has machine learning capabilities — PAL (Predictive Analysis Library) and APL (Automated Predictive Library) provide a solid foundation for classical ML workloads that run close to the data. But these capabilities have a fundamental limitation: they are designed for traditional ML patterns and do not provide a path to deploying modern foundation models, RAG systems, or generative AI against your SAP data in a production-ready, governed way.

SAP AI Core, accessible through BTP and natively integrated with HANA Cloud, is a different category of capability entirely. It is a managed AI runtime that handles the infrastructure, scaling, and lifecycle management of AI workloads — allowing you to focus on the AI logic rather than the infrastructure that runs it.

For SAP architects and data engineers, the critical insight is this: AI Core is where your HANA Cloud data becomes the foundation for genuinely intelligent enterprise applications, not just analytical ones.

Vector Embeddings and RAG for SAP Data

HANA Cloud includes native vector storage capabilities — the ability to store and query vector embeddings directly in the database alongside your structured SAP data. This is the foundational capability for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems that can answer questions about your SAP landscape using live data rather than static training.

The architecture this enables is significant: instead of sending sensitive SAP business data to an external AI service for every query, you store embeddings of your SAP content in HANA Cloud's vector store and retrieve only the relevant context for each query. The foundation model gets the context it needs without your raw SAP data leaving your controlled environment.

-- Create a vector store table in HANA Cloud

CREATE TABLE sap_knowledge_embeddings (

  id          BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,

  content     NCLOB,

  source_type NVARCHAR(100),

  embedding   REAL_VECTOR(1536)

);

-- Semantic similarity search against SAP knowledge base

SELECT TOP 5 id, content, source_type,

  COSINE_SIMILARITY(embedding, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS score

FROM sap_knowledge_embeddings

ORDER BY score DESC;

This pattern — storing SAP process documentation, BAPI descriptions, master data definitions, and business rules as searchable embeddings — is the foundation of a genuine enterprise AI assistant that knows your specific SAP landscape, not just generic SAP knowledge.

Connecting Foundation Models via AI Core APIs

AI Core provides governed, auditable access to foundation models — including SAP's own generative AI models and third-party models through the Generative AI Hub — with the enterprise controls that on-premise environments cannot provide: usage tracking, cost allocation, rate limiting, and compliance logging built in.

The connection between HANA Cloud data and AI Core models enables patterns that were genuinely not achievable on-premise:

  • Intelligent document processing — extracting structured data from unstructured SAP documents (purchase orders, invoices, contracts) using foundation models and storing results directly in HANA Cloud tables.
  • Natural language interfaces to SAP data — allowing business users to query HANA Cloud analytics in plain language, with AI Core translating natural language to SQL and grounding responses in live data.
  • Automated exception handling — AI agents that monitor SAP business processes, identify anomalies in HANA Cloud data, and take corrective actions through SAP APIs without human intervention.
  • Fine-tuned models on SAP data — training or fine-tuning foundation models on your organization's specific SAP data patterns through AI Core's managed training infrastructure.

Grounding AI in Live SAP Business Data

The most powerful aspect of the HANA Cloud and AI Core combination is the ability to ground AI outputs in live, governed SAP business data. Generic AI models hallucinate when asked about your specific business — your customer master, your material catalog, your financial postings. Grounded models, retrieval systems, and tool-calling agents that query HANA Cloud directly produce outputs that are accurate, current, and auditable.

The architecture pattern that is emerging as the standard for enterprise SAP AI: HANA Cloud as the data foundation and vector store → AI Core as the model runtime and orchestration layer → SAP Business AI applications as the user-facing interface. Each layer does what it is best at, and the combination is genuinely more capable than any of its parts.

Capability

What It Unlocks

Native vector storage

Store and query embeddings directly in HANA Cloud alongside structured SAP data

RAG on SAP data

Build retrieval systems that answer questions using live SAP content without external data exposure

Foundation model access

Governed, auditable access to SAP and third-party foundation models through Generative AI Hub

Fine-tuning capability

Train models on your specific SAP data patterns through AI Core's managed infrastructure

Grounded AI outputs

AI responses anchored to live HANA Cloud data — accurate, current, and auditable

Intelligent automation

AI agents that monitor SAP processes and act on HANA data without human intervention

IMPROVEMENT 3 OF 3 

The BTP Application Ecosystem: Connecting HANA Cloud to Everything

From Database to Platform

On-premise HANA is primarily a database — an exceptional one, but a database. Applications connect to it, consume its data, and leave. The integration patterns are well established but fundamentally passive: HANA stores and processes data, applications request it.

HANA Cloud on BTP is part of a platform ecosystem that changes this dynamic. The connections between HANA Cloud and the broader SAP and non-SAP landscape are richer, more standardized, and more capable than what on-premise environments support. Your HANA Cloud instance is not just a database — it is a data foundation for a platform that can build, connect, and deploy enterprise applications at a speed that on-premise infrastructure cannot match.

Connecting HANA Cloud to S/4HANA and ECC

The integration between HANA Cloud and SAP's core ERP systems — S/4HANA and ECC — is one of the most practically valuable improvements for organizations running mixed landscapes. On-premise, cross-system data access required custom RFC connections, ALE configurations, or ETL pipelines that were expensive to build and fragile to maintain.

Through BTP's Integration Suite and HANA Cloud's federation capabilities, S/4HANA and ECC data becomes accessible in HANA Cloud with significantly less custom integration work. This enables analytical and AI use cases that span transactional ERP data and the broader data landscape without requiring full data replication.

  • Real-time operational reporting on S/4HANA data through HANA Cloud without impacting ERP system performance.
  • Cross-system analytics combining ERP data with data from non-SAP sources in a single HANA Cloud analytical layer.
  • AI and ML workloads that consume live ERP data through governed, auditable connections rather than stale data extracts.

Building Fiori and BTP Applications on HANA Cloud Data

SAP Business Application Studio — the cloud-based IDE that replaces the Eclipse-based tooling of on-premise development — provides a first-class development environment for building Fiori applications, CAP (Cloud Application Programming model) services, and full-stack BTP applications directly on top of HANA Cloud data.

The CAP model in particular represents a significant improvement over traditional on-premise application development patterns. CAP services expose HANA Cloud data through OData and REST APIs with built-in authorization, query handling, and SAP Fiori integration — dramatically reducing the boilerplate code required to build enterprise-grade applications on SAP data.

-- CAP service definition exposing HANA Cloud entity

service AnalyticsService {

  @readonly entity SalesOrders as

    SELECT from db.SalesOrders {

      order_id, customer, amount, status,

      created_at, region

    } where status = 'ACTIVE';

}

-- Automatically generates OData V4 endpoint

-- with filtering, sorting, and pagination built in

OData and REST API Exposure

Exposing HANA Cloud data through standardized OData and REST APIs — consumable by Fiori applications, third-party tools, mobile applications, and external systems — is significantly more streamlined on BTP than on-premise. The CAP framework handles the API layer, and BTP's API Management provides governance, rate limiting, and monitoring for those APIs across all consumers.

For organizations building an enterprise data mesh or API-first data strategy, this is one of the most compelling HANA Cloud advantages — your HANA data becomes a governed, discoverable, consumable API product rather than a database that specific applications happen to know how to query.

SAP Integration Suite and Side-by-Side Extensibility

SAP Integration Suite on BTP provides pre-built integration flows between HANA Cloud and the broader SAP and non-SAP ecosystem — Salesforce, Microsoft, ServiceNow, and hundreds of other systems — without custom middleware development. On-premise integration required custom development, dedicated middleware servers, and ongoing maintenance of bespoke integration code.

Side-by-side extensibility — the BTP pattern for extending S/4HANA and other SAP systems without modifying core — relies on HANA Cloud as its data persistence layer. Extension applications built on BTP consume S/4HANA data through governed APIs, process and store results in HANA Cloud, and surface insights back to SAP users through Fiori — all without touching the core ERP system.

Side-by-side extensibility is the pattern that makes HANA Cloud most valuable for organizations that need to innovate on top of S/4HANA without the risk and cost of core modifications. HANA Cloud is the data foundation that makes this pattern production-ready.

Capability

What It Unlocks

S/4HANA and ECC integration

Cross-system analytics and AI workloads on live ERP data without custom RFC development

CAP application development

Build Fiori and full-stack BTP applications on HANA Cloud data with dramatically less boilerplate

OData and REST APIs

Expose HANA Cloud data as governed, discoverable API products consumable by any client

SAP Integration Suite

Pre-built integration flows to SAP and non-SAP systems without custom middleware

Side-by-side extensibility

Extend S/4HANA through BTP applications backed by HANA Cloud without core modifications

BTP ecosystem

Access to the full BTP service catalog — AI, analytics, integration, automation — from a single platform

The Honest Summary

The three improvements covered in this article are not incremental upgrades to what on-premise HANA already does. They are qualitatively different capabilities that change what is possible for development teams, data engineers, and enterprise architects working with SAP data.

HDI containers with Git integration bring database development into the modern software development workflow — version controlled, isolated, reviewable, and reproducible. SAP AI Core unlocks production-grade AI on your SAP data — RAG systems, foundation model access, intelligent automation — that simply cannot be built with the same reliability and governance on-premise. And the BTP application ecosystem transforms HANA Cloud from a database into a platform foundation for the full range of SAP and cross-system enterprise applications.

None of this makes the migration easy. Part 2 of this series is honest about the friction — and there is real friction. But the capabilities waiting on the other side of that friction are substantial enough to make the journey worth taking.

Part 2 of this series covers the pain points in detail: HANA Studio to Cloud Central tooling gaps, stored procedure compatibility issues, and security model changes that will catch your team off guard. Read both before finalizing your migration plan.

This is what SAP displays in VA02. However, the user expects that it will be sorted according to level.

LEVELPOSNRUEPOS
4000010
1200000
22050020000
32050120500
1400000
1600000

Expected output:

LEVELPOSNRUEPOS
1200000
22050020000
32050120500
40000120501
1400000
1600000
